headline: BaseballPro77 Rewrites the Record Books in a Week the League Won't Soon Forget
subheadline: Mike Trout's video-game performance powered the most dominant offensive week in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball history — and the gap at the top is only getting wider.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Canberra Wombats vs. Riverside Roughnecks — 4-4 Tie

In a league where every category point carries playoff implications, the only result more frustrating than a loss is a tie that feels like one — and that's precisely what both **Canberra Wombats** and **Riverside Roughnecks** walked away with after one of the most evenly contested matchups of the young season. The Wombats posted 30 runs, six home runs, and a .283 OBP, while Riverside countered with a .368 OBP and eight net saves, but neither team could find the separation that would have made the difference. **Canberra's** Corbin Carroll was held in check offensively and Henderson's .189 OBP continues to be a structural drag on a lineup that needs its cornerstone players producing. **Riverside**, meanwhile, left saves on the table in a matchup they needed — their 8 NSVH was the highest in the league this week, and they still couldn't close it out. Both teams remain squarely in the muddled middle of the standings heading into Week 5, which means a clean win next week isn't just desirable — it's urgent.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

There are performances in fantasy baseball that defy easy categorization — weeks so statistically overwhelming that they force you to recalibrate your understanding of what a single roster can accomplish. Week 4 in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball produced exactly that kind of moment, and it arrived courtesy of **BaseballPro77** and a Mike Trout performance that looked less like a fantasy baseball week and more like a cheat code being entered in real time.

Trout posted six home runs, 11 RBI, and a .459 OBP across the week, a singularly absurd line that, on its own, would have carried most rosters to a competitive finish. But Trout wasn't operating in isolation — he was the centerpiece of a **BaseballPro77** offensive output that shattered two league records simultaneously. The team's 19 home runs surpassed the previous single-week high in league history, and the 39 RBI established a new benchmark that, given the context of a 14-team league, represents a kind of offensive critical mass that few fantasy teams ever achieve. To be clear about the scope of what happened: 19 HR and 39 RBI in a single week means **BaseballPro77** averaged better than 2.7 home runs and 5.5 RBI every single day. That is not a hot streak. That is a freight train.

The **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** drew the short straw as the opponent in this particular historical moment, managing just 33 hits, a .257 OBP, and a 5.01 ERA in a 7-2 loss that was never as close as that score implies. The Reapers do have a genuine piece in Ben Rice, but the gulf between what **BaseballPro77** is doing offensively and what most of this league is producing is becoming a structural concern for the field. **BaseballPro77's** pitching held a 2.33 ERA on the week, meaning this wasn't a team that traded defensive categories for offensive ones — they won across the board. Combined with **General Hospital's** league-leading 8 category wins and 2.68 ERA, the message from the top two teams in this league is becoming increasingly clear: everyone else is playing for third.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### BaseballPro77 def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-2

The numbers tell the story more efficiently than most prose could. **BaseballPro77** posted 19 HR, 39 RBI, 39 runs, 71 strikeouts from the pitching staff, and a 2.33 ERA — a complete-game demolition across both offensive and pitching categories. The **Reapers**, for their part, managed 27 runs, eight home runs, and a .257 OBP, which represented something close to a roster-wide swing-and-miss at the plate. Ben Rice remains a legitimate building block for **Wisconsin Rapids**, and their 9 NSVH was actually the highest in the league — a silver lining that will be cold comfort in the standings. The more pressing concern for the **Reapers** is an OBP figure that ranked last in the league this week, signaling that the problem is less about individual effort and more about roster construction. Until they address the on-base deficiencies across the lineup, weeks like this will become a pattern rather than an outlier.

### General Hospital def. Benchwarmers, 8-2

**General Hospital** delivered the cleanest performance in the league this week, winning eight of ten categories in a wire-to-wire statement win over the **Benchwarmers**. Their 77.1 innings pitched led the league, their 2.68 ERA was elite, and the addition of Kumar Rocker to an already formidable rotation signals that this manager is not content to rest on the best pitching staff in the league — they want to extend the gap. The **Benchwarmers** were carried almost entirely by Michael King's remarkable pitching performance, but when your offense posts just four home runs and 45 strikeouts, the burden on any single arm becomes impossibly heavy. **General Hospital's** WHIP of 0.96 was the best in the league, and when you combine that kind of pitching with a lineup that posted a .339 OBP and 10 HR, you have a team that is genuinely difficult to attack from any angle. The **Benchwarmers** should be grateful for the 8-point NSVH cushion they accumulated — without it, this could have been far worse.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. North Correa, 5-2

**Athletic Park Dragons** continued their ascent up the power rankings with a 5-2 decision over **North Correa**, powered by a jaw-dropping performance from Aaron Judge, who went deep six times on the week. The Dragons posted 13 HR, 36 runs, 70 strikeouts from the pitching staff, and a 3.01 ERA — a well-rounded effort that reflects a roster with genuine depth at multiple positions. **North Correa** was not without their own weapons: Chris Sale posted a 0.69 ERA, the team accumulated five quality starts, and the Ohtani-Hoerner combination gives them a legitimate multi-category floor. The problem for **North Correa** was pitching volume — 56.2 innings pitched was among the lower totals in the league, and only 54 strikeouts from the staff left categories on the table. Their new acquisitions of Austin Wells and Brayan Rocchio show a manager who understands the roster's structural needs, and heading into Week 5, there's reason to believe this team gets closer to its ceiling.

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 5-4

In what might be the most analytically interesting matchup of the week, the **Albuquerque Bombers** posted a 4.03 ERA and won — because they outscored the **Skeeters** significantly in offensive categories. **Sugar Land** actually had the edge in strikeouts (72 to 59) and quality starts (4 to 3), but their pitching ERA of 6.05 was the worst in the league this week, and that kind of number makes wins all but impossible to hold. **Albuquerque** got an enormous lift from Byron Buxton, who homered three times, and the team's 7 stolen bases were a category swing in a game this tight. For the **Skeeters**, this is the kind of loss that hurts more in hindsight than in real time — they remain one of the most consistently constructed rosters in the league, and Fernando Tatis Jr., Ian Happ, and Austin Weathers give them multi-category upside on any given night. A stumble this early in the season is not a crisis, but it's a reminder that consistency alone doesn't guarantee wins in a category-scoring format.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Hiroshima Radiation, 6-4

José Ramírez and Yordan Alvarez each went deep four times this week, and **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** still needed a combination of pitching misfires from their opponent to secure the 6-4 win. The **Twins** generated 14 HR, 37 runs, and 8 stolen bases — the kind of offensive output that would win most matchups in this league handily. But their pitching posted a 5.60 ERA and just two quality starts, which meant the margin for error was razor-thin. **Hiroshima Radiation** pitched extraordinarily well — a 2.81 ERA, five quality starts, and a 1.09 WHIP — but managed just six home runs and one stolen base on offense, categories that cost them dearly in the final tally. The **Radiation** are a team that pitches like a playoff contender and hits like a team hoping to sneak into the conversation, and until that imbalance is addressed, losses like this will continue to accumulate.

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Buttville Skidmarks, 6-3

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** did what they do best: pitch their way to a comfortable win. Their league-leading 8 quality starts, 2.49 ERA, and 0.98 WHIP are becoming the most reliable combination of numbers in this league, and Paul Skenes' 0.90 ERA remains one of the genuinely elite individual pitching performances week over week. The **Wonderdogs** also posted a .359 OBP — the best offensive on-base figure in this matchup — which gave their lineup just enough of an edge to complement the pitching staff's dominance. **Buttville Skidmarks** logged 77.2 innings pitched, which was the highest volume in the league this week, but a 4.87 ERA meant that volume hurt more than it helped. Matt Reynolds posted a .500 OBP, which is the kind of individual performance that deserves recognition — but when it exists in isolation on a roster with structural problems, it becomes a footnote rather than a foundation.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers**

There is a version of Week 4 in which the **Benchwarmers** lose by six or seven categories. They posted just four home runs — the fewest in the league — accumulated only 45 strikeouts from the pitching staff, and finished bottom-five in most offensive categories. That they managed to win two categories against a **General Hospital** team that was firing on all cylinders is, in large part, a credit to Michael King's exceptional work. But the **Benchwarmers** carry the league's highest luck delta at +1.21, which is a number that deserves serious attention. Luck deltas don't lie — they tell you where a team is overperforming relative to its actual production, and a gap that large suggests regression is not a question of if but when. Unless this roster sees significant additions in the coming weeks, the underlying numbers will eventually catch up with the results.

**Unlucky Team: Abbotsford-Colby Twins**

By any objective measure of weekly talent deployment, the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** should be sitting comfortably in the upper tier of this standings. Ramírez posted a .500 OBP with four home runs and five stolen bases. Alvarez added four more home runs. The team won six categories. And yet the **Twins** carry the league's worst luck delta at -0.61, a persistent gap between the talent on their roster and the wins that talent is generating. That kind of sustained misalignment — where legitimate production keeps failing to convert into the results it deserves — is one of the more frustrating phenomena in category-scoring fantasy baseball. The **Twins** are not a bad team. In fact, they may have a top-five roster on paper. The standings, for now, refuse to reflect it.

---

## League Records

**BaseballPro77** made history in Week 4, breaking two league records simultaneously. Their 19 home runs in a single week surpassed the previous league mark, and their 39 RBI established a new high-water line that the league has never seen. Both records fell in the same week, against the same opponent, powered in significant part by one player performing at an MVP level that compressed the gap between individual brilliance and team dominance into something nearly indistinguishable. The previous records had stood as benchmarks for what a weekly ceiling looked like in this league. **BaseballPro77** erased both in a single afternoon.

---

## Manager Spotlight: North Correa

**North Correa** sits seventh in the current power rankings, a position that feels both accurate and slightly undersell of what this roster is capable of when everything clicks. Their 5-2 loss to **Athletic Park Dragons** this week stings, but the underlying architecture of this team is more interesting than the record suggests. Chris Sale's 0.69 ERA this week was one of the elite individual pitching performances in the league, and the Shohei Ohtani and Nico Hoerner combination gives **North Correa** a dynamic offensive floor that few teams in the middle of the rankings can match.

What's more noteworthy, however, is the managerial activity on the waiver wire. Dropping an aging J.T. Realmuto in favor of the breakout Austin Wells at catcher reflects an understanding that fantasy value doesn't follow reputation — it follows production. Wells is one of the more interesting young catchers in baseball right now, and getting ahead of that curve is exactly the kind of forward-thinking move that separates well-managed rosters from static ones. The addition of Brayan Rocchio addresses a genuine middle-infield depth need, and while neither addition is a headline move, the accumulation of smart, targeted decisions is how championships are quietly assembled.

The path to the playoffs for **North Correa** runs through their pitching volume. If Sale remains healthy and the rotation deepens, and if Ohtani's offensive contributions remain consistent across categories, this team has the ceiling to push into the top five. The Week 5 schedule will be instructive — if they can bounce back against a winnable opponent, the 5-2 loss starts to look like a stumble rather than a pattern.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Mike Trout (BaseballPro77):** Trout delivered six home runs, 11 RBI, and a .459 OBP in a single week — the kind of performance that doesn't just win matchups, it wins arguments. **Verdict: Hold.** There is no world in which you move a player who just carried your roster to two simultaneous league records. Unless a historically transformative offer arrives, Trout is untouchable.

**José Ramírez (Abbotsford-Colby Twins):** Ramírez posted a .500 OBP, four home runs, and five stolen bases this week, and his team still finished with a -0.61 luck delta dragging down the results. **Verdict: Buy.** If you can acquire Ramírez from a frustrated **Twins** manager who keeps watching elite production fail to translate into wins, you are acquiring one of the two or three most complete offensive players in this league at a potential discount.

**Michael King (Benchwarmers):** King's 19-strikeout week was genuinely elite pitching, and his contributions represent the primary reason the **Benchwarmers** aren't sitting at the bottom of the standings outright. **Verdict: Sell.** A team with a +1.21 luck delta and bottom-five offense is a team in structural decline. An interested manager should open conversations now, before the regression hits and the asking price collapses along with the roster around him.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **BaseballPro77** | Set league records in HR and RBI, Trout is in full MVP mode, and the pitching held a 2.33 ERA — no one else is playing at this level right now [SAME]
2. **General Hospital** | Led the league with 8 category wins, 77.1 IP, 6 QS, and a 2.68 ERA; added Kumar Rocker to an already deep rotation that is becoming truly frightening [SAME]
3. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Dropped a 5-4 decision but remain the most consistent team in the league across all four weeks; Tatis, Happ, and Weathers give them multi-category upside nightly [SAME]
4. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Judge's 6-HR week was jaw-dropping and Sánchez/Ober both delivered; this team is rounding into a genuine contender with pitching depth to match the offense [SAME]
5. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | A league-leading 8 QS and 2.49 ERA remain the identity; Skenes' 0.90 ERA and smart waiver additions like Masyn Winn show an active, forward-thinking manager [SAME]
6. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Ramirez and Alvarez each went off for 4 HR and the team won 6 categories — the talent is unquestionable, but a -0.61 luck delta is a brutal ongoing tax on a top-five roster [UP]
7. **North Correa** | Chris Sale's 0.69 ERA anchored the week and the Ohtani-Hoerner combo gives them a high floor; Wells and Rocchio upgrades at catcher and middle infield show smart roster management [SAME]
8. **Hiroshima Radiation** | McLean and Eovaldi combined for 5 QS and sub-2.00 ERAs again, but a -0.39 luck delta and an offense that managed just 6 HR and 1 SB are real ceiling concerns [DOWN]
9. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Buxton's 3-HR week sparked a 5-4 win and the roster is actively being patched with four waiver additions; the activity is encouraging but the holes are real [SAME]
10. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Tied when a win looked probable; Langeliers, Kurtz, and Misiorowski are legitimate assets but the team still can't string enough categories together consistently [SAME]
11. **Canberra Wombats** | A tie beats a loss but Henderson's .189 OBP is alarming for a cornerstone player; the pitching inventory is thin and the Week 5 draw against Wonder Wharf is a tough ask [SAME]
12. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Got steamrolled 7-2 by the league's hottest team; Ben Rice is a real piece but 33 hits and a .257 OBP league-wide signal a roster that needs significant offensive upgrades [DOWN]
13. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Reynolds posted a .500 OBP again and it meant nothing; the pitching posted a 4.87 ERA and the team managed just 3 category wins — the same structural problems, the same result [SAME]
14. **Benchwarmers** | Michael King's 19 K effort is elite and real, but a +1.21 luck delta masking 4 HR, 45 K, and bottom-five offense means regression is coming fast unless major additions are made [SAME]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[A] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Masyn Winn (SS), Dustin May (SP), and Scott Barlow (RP); dropped corresponding roster fillers — A fully loaded move that adds positional flexibility, rotation depth, and bullpen help simultaneously. This is what dynasty-building looks like in real time: not one splashy acquisition, but three targeted additions that address three distinct roster needs in a single week. A manager who is already running the league's best pitching staff just made it deeper.

**[A-] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker (SP); dropped Leiter, Junk, and Javier — Cleaning house on underperforming arms and upgrading to a high-ceiling starter is exactly what a first-place team should do to protect its lead. The willingness to cut sunk costs without sentiment is a hallmark of championship roster management, and Rocker gives a rotation that was already formidable another legitimate weapon.

**[B+] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Keaton Winn, Braxton Garrett, Noelvi Marte, and Josh Jung; dropped four roster fillers — Four additions in one week signals urgency, and urgency at this stage of the season is both appropriate and encouraging. Jung and Marte add lineup versatility, and the starting pitching depth directly addresses the area most likely to cost this team in close matchups. The volume of activity is a positive sign that this manager
